questions.question: Which of the four recovery experiences has the strongest link to reduced fatigue?

options.text: Relaxation
Mastery
Control over leisure time
Psychological detachment from work

questions.explanation: Psychological detachment — mentally switching off from work and not thinking about tasks, emails, or problems during off-hours — has the strongest negative association with fatigue. When you keep thinking about work, your stress response stays active even though you are physically off.

questions.question: Why does the lesson say that scrolling social media often does not count as true relaxation?

options.text: Because social media is addictive and should be avoided entirely
Because it often increases arousal rather than reduces it — true relaxation leaves you feeling calmer, not more stimulated
Because it counts as work since you are using a screen
Because it prevents you from sleeping immediately afterward

questions.explanation: Scrolling social media or watching intense TV may feel like relaxation, but they often increase arousal rather than reduce it. True relaxation directly counteracts the physical effects of stress — lowering heart rate, blood pressure, and muscle tension. It should leave you feeling calmer, not more stimulated.

questions.question: Why does rest feel so hard when you are burned out?

options.text: Because rest is physically painful during burnout
Because of guilt, anxiety from stopping, wired-but-tired nervous system, identity tied to productivity, and loss of ability to enjoy things
Because you have not earned rest until all your work is done
Because burnout makes you incapable of sitting still

questions.explanation: Rest feels hard during burnout for multiple reasons: guilt ('I should be productive'), anxiety (stopping means confronting feelings you have been outrunning), being wired-but-tired, identity tied to productivity making rest feel threatening, and anhedonia (loss of ability to enjoy things). The lesson recommends starting with very small doses.

questions.question: Why is planned, regular micro-recovery more effective than occasional long vacations?

options.text: Because vacations are too expensive to be practical
Because vacation benefits typically fade within 2-4 weeks, while regular micro-recovery sustains ongoing wellbeing
Because vacations cause more stress from travel and planning
Because employers prefer employees who take shorter breaks

questions.explanation: Research shows that planned, regular micro-recovery is more effective for ongoing wellbeing than occasional long vacations, whose benefits typically fade within 2-4 weeks. Daily, evening, weekend, and seasonal recovery practices create sustained protection against burnout.

questions.question: What does the lesson say about waiting until you 'feel like it' to start a leisure activity during burnout?

options.text: You should always wait for motivation before starting an activity
Motivation is not relevant to recovery — only rest matters
Do not wait — motivation follows action, not the other way around, especially during burnout
You need at least a week of rest before trying any activity

questions.explanation: When you are burned out, motivation is one of the first things to go. Research shows that motivation follows action, not the other way around. Starting a small, low-pressure activity often generates the enjoyment and energy that felt impossible beforehand. Do not wait until you feel like it.

title: Recovery Practices: Rest, Play, and Disconnection - Quiz
